question_text: A student observes that when white light passes through a blue filter, the transmitted light appears blue. Which of the following best explains why red light does not appear in the transmitted light?

options.label: A
options.text: The blue filter converts red light into blue light through refraction

options.label: B
options.text: The blue filter absorbs red light because red light waves have a different wavelength that the filter cannot transmit

options.label: C
options.text: Red light is scattered by the filter material before it can pass through

options.label: D
options.text: The blue filter reflects all colours except blue, which passes straight through

correct_answer: The blue filter absorbs red light because red light waves have a different wavelength that the filter cannot transmit
explanation: Colour filters work by absorbing certain wavelengths of light while transmitting others. A blue filter transmits blue light because the wavelength of blue light matches what the filter allows through. Red light has a different wavelength, so the filter material absorbs it rather than letting it pass through. This is why we only see blue light coming out of the filter.
